title: SEGMENT 10 — Graft-versus-host disease: the central chronic complication

lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — Graft-versus-host disease: the central chronic complication
Welcome back.
In the second half of this lecture we turn to the complications and the modern era.
And the place to start, after a break, is the single most feared complication of allogeneic HSCT: graft-versus-host disease, abbreviated GVHD.
GVHD is the inverse of organ rejection.
In a kidney transplant, the recipient's immune system attacks the foreign graft.

In an allogeneic HSCT, the recipient's immune system has been ablated by conditioning, so the donor's immune system — which came with the transplanted stem cells — wakes up in a new body and attacks the recipient's tissues.
The donor T cells see the recipient's organs, recognize their HLA shapes as foreign, and mount an immune assault.
The graft attacks the host.
That's the disease.
GVHD comes in two clinical phenotypes, defined by timing and pattern.
Acute GVHD typically presents in the first 100 days after transplant.

It targets three organs: the skin (rash, often starting on the palms and soles and spreading), the gut (severe diarrhea, abdominal pain, nausea), and the liver (cholestatic hepatitis with rising bilirubin).
It's graded I through IV based on severity.
Grade I is mild skin rash, manageable with topical steroids.
Grade IV is multi-organ failure, often fatal.
Severe acute GVHD is one of the main causes of treatment-related mortality after allogeneic transplant.
Chronic GVHD typically presents later — months to years post-transplant — and is more like an autoimmune disease.
It can affect skin (thickening, scleroderma-like changes), eyes (dry eye), mouth (lichen planus-like lesions), lungs (bronchiolitis obliterans), joints, GI tract, liver, and genitourinary tract.
Chronic GVHD is the leading cause of late morbidity in transplant survivors.
Patients can live for decades with chronic GVHD requiring ongoing immunosuppression, with significant quality-of-life impact.
The standard GVHD prophylaxis is a calcineurin inhibitor — cyclosporine or tacrolimus — combined with methotrexate in the early post-transplant days.
This dates from the work of Storb and colleagues in Seattle in the 1980s and has been the backbone of GVHD prevention ever since.

Treatment of established GVHD starts with high-dose corticosteroids, and for refractory cases involves drugs like ruxolitinib (a JAK inhibitor approved for steroid-refractory GVHD), ibrutinib, extracorporeal photopheresis, and many others.
The therapeutic landscape for GVHD has expanded dramatically in the last decade.
GVHD is the price of allogeneic transplant.
Reducing it without losing the graft-versus-tumor effect — the silver lining we'll cover next — is the central unsolved problem of the field.
